name: Gawker Media
summary: A network of irreverent blogs (Gawker, Deadspin, Jezebel, Gizmodo) built a large audience but was forced into bankruptcy after a $140M jury verdict in Hulk Hogan's Peter Thiel-funded invasion-of-privacy lawsuit.
lesson: A single unresolved legal liability, especially one bankrolled by a hostile billionaire, can bankrupt an otherwise profitable media company overnight.

name: BuzzFeed News
summary: BuzzFeed's Pulitzer Prize-winning news division was shut down in 2023 as parent company BuzzFeed Inc, which had gone public via SPAC, cut costs after its stock collapsed.
lesson: Prestige journalism can win awards while still being the least profitable division a public company sacrifices first.

name: Mic
summary: A millennial-focused news site chased Facebook video pivots, laid off most editorial staff in 2017 after a traffic collapse, and was sold for parts in 2019.
lesson: Rebuilding an entire editorial strategy around a single platform's algorithm leaves no fallback when that algorithm changes.

name: The Awl
summary: A beloved independent blog network (The Awl, The Hairpin) known for idiosyncratic writing shut down in 2018 after its small ad-based business could no longer sustain operations.
lesson: Critical acclaim and a devoted readership don't guarantee a display-ad business model can cover the bills.

name: Gigaom
summary: A respected tech-industry news and research site abruptly shut down in 2015 after failing to raise further financing to cover mounting debts.
lesson: A trusted editorial brand can still collapse suddenly if working capital runs out faster than a financing round can close.

name: Grantland
summary: Bill Simmons' ESPN-backed sports and pop-culture site built a devoted following but was shuttered after Simmons' contract with ESPN was not renewed.
lesson: A media brand built tightly around one star founder's voice is existentially exposed to that founder's relationship with the parent company.

name: Circa
summary: A mobile-first news app that broke stories into structured, updatable facts shut down in 2015 after parent company Sinclair Broadcast Group declined to continue funding it.
lesson: An innovative story format is not enough if the corporate parent funding it doesn't see a path to a sustaining business model.

name: Ozy Media
summary: A digital media and events company collapsed in 2021 after a New York Times investigation revealed executives had impersonated a YouTube executive on a fundraising call and inflated audience metrics.
lesson: Fabricating audience metrics to raise capital eventually collides with due diligence, and the collapse can be immediate and total.

name: The Daily
summary: News Corp's iPad-only newspaper, championed by Rupert Murdoch, shut down after just under two years due to weak subscriber growth relative to its high production costs.
lesson: Betting an entire publication's distribution on a single device's adoption curve is a fragile strategy.

name: Storify
summary: A social-media curation tool that let journalists compile tweets into stories was acquired by Livefyre, then Adobe, and shut down in 2018 as usage declined.
lesson: A tool built around one platform's embed API is vulnerable when that platform changes access or the workflow it served falls out of fashion.

name: Newsvine
summary: An early citizen-journalism and social-news site was acquired by MSNBC, gradually deprioritized, and formally shut down in 2017.
lesson: Citizen-journalism platforms without a growth strategy were largely displaced once social networks absorbed that function.

name: Patch (AOL)
summary: AOL's ambitious network of hundreds of hyperlocal news sites was gutted in 2013-2014 with mass layoffs and site closures after failing to prove a sustainable local-ad revenue model at scale.
lesson: Scaling a local-news format to hundreds of towns simultaneously outpaces the ability to prove unit economics work in even one.

name: Vice Media
summary: Once valued at $5.7B, the youth-media empire filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after years of missed revenue targets, executive turnover, and an inability to translate buzz into profit.
lesson: A soaring private valuation built on growth narratives can mask a core business that was never actually profitable.

name: Vocativ
summary: A data-driven digital news outlet backed by Jigsaw Capital shut down abruptly in 2018 after burning through funding without reaching sustainable revenue.
lesson: Investing heavily in proprietary research and technology for journalism doesn't guarantee a monetizable audience results.

name: DNAinfo
summary: Billionaire Joe Ricketts abruptly shut down hyperlocal news sites DNAinfo and Gothamist just a week after their newsrooms voted to unionize, laying off around 115 staffers.
lesson: A media outlet fully dependent on one wealthy owner's goodwill can be shut down for reasons unrelated to its underlying business performance.

name: Splinter
funding: Part of Gizmodo Media Group/G-O Media
summary: A politics and culture site spun out of Fusion was shut down by G/O Media in 2019 as the private-equity-backed owner consolidated and cut underperforming Gawker-descended brands.
lesson: Private-equity ownership of media brands tends to prioritize consolidation and cost-cutting over incubating smaller editorial voices.

name: The Outline
summary: A design-forward digital publication founded by Josh Topolsky ceased original editorial operations in 2020 after failing to build a sustainable advertising and subscription business.
lesson: Distinctive design and a strong editorial voice can build critical buzz without solving the underlying revenue problem.

name: Xconomy
summary: A regional technology and biotech business news network covering multiple U.S. innovation hubs shut down in 2022, citing an unsustainable local-media business model.
lesson: Niche regional business journalism faces a smaller ad market that often cannot support a multi-city network long-term.

name: Al Jazeera America
summary: Qatar-backed Al Jazeera's U.S. cable and digital news channel shut down after less than three years due to low ratings and an inability to secure widespread cable distribution.
lesson: Heavy capital investment cannot overcome a structurally difficult distribution environment for a new entrant news brand.

name: Deadspin (original)
summary: After G/O Media ordered the sports-and-culture site to 'stick to sports,' the entire editorial staff resigned in protest in 2019, effectively gutting the brand under its private-equity owner.
lesson: Editorial staff who built a brand's voice can walk away en masse when new ownership tries to override that identity.

name: Jezebel
summary: A prominent feminist pop-culture site descended from Gawker Media was shut down by G/O Media in 2023 citing declining traffic and revenue, before being revived by Paste Magazine in 2024.
lesson: A recognizable, influential media brand can still be discarded by a cost-cutting owner if its standalone numbers don't clear the bar.

name: Demand Media (eHow)
summary: A content farm that used algorithms to mass-produce SEO-optimized articles (eHow, Cracked) went public in 2011, then saw its stock collapse after Google's Panda algorithm update devalued its content, leading to a split into Leaf Group.
lesson: A business model built entirely around gaming a third-party search algorithm is fundamentally at that platform's mercy.

name: Say Media
summary: Formerly VideoEgg, this media and technology company pivoted repeatedly through video advertising, a blog network, and a content-management platform before winding down operations in 2017.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots without landing on a durable one eventually exhausts investor patience and cash.

name: Federated Media Publishing
summary: An early blog-advertising network that represented influential bloggers for brand deals wound down operations around 2015 as native advertising shifted to in-house platform tools.
lesson: Ad-network intermediaries between advertisers and independent creators get squeezed out once platforms build direct monetization tools.

name: NewsCred
summary: A content-marketing platform that licensed news content and built enterprise content workflow tools sold its licensing business and shut down after burning through significant venture funding.
lesson: Pivoting from content licensing to enterprise SaaS marketing tools didn't resolve an underlying inability to reach profitability.

name: LittleThings
summary: A lifestyle and feel-good content site that built enormous traffic through Facebook shut down abruptly in 2018 after a Facebook News Feed algorithm change collapsed its referral traffic overnight.
lesson: Building a media business almost entirely on a single platform's referral traffic leaves no cushion when that platform deprioritizes publishers.

name: Byliner
summary: A digital publisher of long-form nonfiction e-singles by notable authors failed to build a sustainable subscription business and effectively ceased operations by 2014.
lesson: Premium long-form digital content struggled to find enough paying subscribers before ad-free bundles like Kindle Singles matured.

name: Quartz
funding: Sold multiple times; last sold for reported ~$1
summary: A business-news outlet acclaimed for its mobile-first design was sold by Atlantic Media to Uzabase, then to G/O Media for a nominal price in 2023 after years of losses, before being resold again in 2024.
lesson: Editorial innovation and design acclaim don't insulate a digital publisher from being sold for parts once losses persist for years.

name: Blendle
summary: A Dutch 'iTunes for news' platform that let readers pay per article for premium journalism failed to reach scale and pivoted away from its original micropayment model before effectively shutting down.
lesson: Micropayments for individual news articles have repeatedly struggled against readers' preference for either free content or flat subscriptions.

name: Capital New York
summary: A New York City politics and media trade publication was acquired by Politico in 2014 and folded into Politico New York in 2015, ending its standalone brand.
lesson: Regional trade publications with a niche but small audience are natural acquisition and consolidation targets rather than standalone survivors.

name: Mahalo.com
summary: Jason Calacanis' human-edited search and content site pivoted repeatedly (search, video courses, HowTo.com) after Google's Panda update devalued its SEO-driven content pages, eventually shutting down.
lesson: A content strategy optimized primarily for search ranking rather than reader value is fragile to search-algorithm changes.

name: Newsweek (IBT Media era)
funding: Sold for $1 in 2010, later relaunched under IBT Media
summary: After decades as a leading American newsweekly, Newsweek was sold by the Washington Post Company for $1 in 2010, ceased its print edition in 2012-2013, and its later owner IBT Media faced fraud investigations tied to Olivet University.
lesson: A storied legacy media brand can be run into serious financial and ethical trouble by owners chasing traffic and dubious ties over sustainable journalism.

name: Genius (Rap Genius)
summary: A crowdsourced lyrics-annotation platform raised significant venture funding at a media-company valuation but repeatedly laid off staff from 2023 onward after failing to build a business matching that valuation.
lesson: A community-driven content platform with huge engagement can still fail to convert that engagement into venture-scale revenue.

name: Homicide Watch
summary: An award-winning hyperlocal crime-reporting site covering every homicide in Washington, D.C. shut down in 2014 after its founders could not build a financially sustainable independent operation.
lesson: Critically acclaimed, civically valuable local journalism can still fail commercially without a scalable revenue model.

name: AllThingsD
summary: Kara Swisher and Walt Mossberg's influential tech-news site under The Wall Street Journal ended in 2013 after a contract dispute with Dow Jones, prompting the founders to depart and launch Re/code.
lesson: A media brand's value is often inseparable from its star journalists, who can walk and rebuild elsewhere when contract terms sour.

name: Gothamist (original, DNAinfo era)
summary: A well-regarded New York City news and culture blog was acquired by Joe Ricketts' DNAinfo in 2017, then shut down alongside DNAinfo just months later after the newsroom unionized, before being revived by New York Public Radio in 2018.
lesson: Acquisition by an owner hostile to unionization can end a media brand's operation almost as quickly as the deal that created it.

name: Mode Media
summary: Formerly Glam Media, this lifestyle content and ad-tech network raised over $300M but shut down abruptly in 2016 after failing to find a buyer amid an unsustainable cash burn rate.
lesson: Raising hundreds of millions to build a content network doesn't guarantee an exit if the underlying ad-tech economics never worked.

name: Refinery29
funding: $130M+ raised; acquired by Vice Media in 2019 for ~$400M
summary: A women's lifestyle and fashion digital publisher built a large, loyal audience and video business, and was acquired by Vice Media Group in 2019 in a deal valuing it near $400M before later moving to Bustle Digital Group.
lesson: A tightly defined audience niche with strong brand loyalty can command a premium acquisition even in a crowded lifestyle-media market.

name: Business Insider
summary: A digital-first business news outlet built rapid scale through aggressive SEO and social distribution, and was acquired by Axel Springer in 2015 in a deal valuing the company at roughly $442M.
lesson: Early, disciplined focus on distribution channels like SEO and social can let a digital-native outlet outgrow legacy competitors quickly.

name: HuffPost (The Huffington Post)
summary: An opinion-and-news aggregation site built massive traffic through unpaid blogger contributions and SEO, and was acquired by AOL in 2011 for $315M, later passing to Verizon and then BuzzFeed.
lesson: Aggregating free contributor content at scale, alongside original reporting, can build enormous traffic and a lucrative acquisition outcome.

name: Axios
funding: $65M+ raised; Cox Enterprises took majority stake in 2022 (~$525M valuation)
summary: Founded by Politico veterans, Axios built a distinctive 'smart brevity' format and newsletter-driven distribution, growing into a profitable news business before Cox Enterprises acquired a majority stake in 2022.
lesson: A sharply differentiated content format and disciplined newsletter distribution can build a profitable news business faster than chasing raw pageviews.

name: The Athletic
funding: $140M raised; acquired by The New York Times in 2022 for $550M
summary: A subscription-only sports journalism site built a large paid subscriber base by hiring local beat reporters away from newspapers, and was acquired by The New York Times in 2022 for $550M.
lesson: A pure-subscription model with high-quality niche journalism can build durable recurring revenue that a legacy publisher will pay a premium to acquire.

name: The Information
summary: Jessica Lessin's subscription-only tech-industry journalism outlet built a profitable, independent business through high-priced subscriptions and scoops, without relying on advertising.
lesson: A high-price, high-value subscription model targeting a professional niche audience can sustain an independent newsroom without ad revenue or outside funding.

name: Substack
summary: A newsletter publishing platform let independent writers charge readers directly for subscriptions, becoming a major venue for journalists and writers leaving traditional outlets to build independent businesses.
lesson: Giving individual writers direct payment infrastructure and audience ownership can unlock a large market of independent publishers traditional media couldn't retain.

name: Morning Brew
funding: Bootstrapped; acquired majority stake by Insider Inc in 2020 (~$75M)
summary: A business-news newsletter built a large, young, engaged subscriber base with a conversational tone and referral-driven growth, and sold a majority stake to Insider Inc in 2020 for a reported $75M.
lesson: A distinctive editorial voice combined with a low-cost referral growth loop can scale a newsletter into a major acquisition target without heavy venture funding.

name: The Skimm
summary: A daily news-digest newsletter aimed at young women built a large subscriber base and expanded into apps, live events, and e-commerce partnerships, becoming a profitable independent media company.
lesson: Deep understanding of a specific underserved audience's daily habits can be turned into a durable multi-format media brand.

name: Politico
funding: Privately held; acquired by Axel Springer in 2021 for $1B
summary: A political-news outlet built a dominant position among Washington insiders through a premium subscription product (Politico Pro) and fast-turnaround reporting, and was acquired by Axel Springer in 2021 for about $1B.
lesson: Building a high-margin premium subscription product for a professional niche audience can be far more valuable than a mass-market ad-supported model.

name: Vox Media
summary: A digital media company built a portfolio of vertical-focused brands (The Verge, Vox, SB Nation, Eater) on its shared Chorus publishing platform, later merging with Group Nine Media in 2024 to form a larger combined company.
lesson: Investing in a shared proprietary publishing and ad-tech platform across multiple editorial brands can create durable operating leverage other digital publishers lack.

name: Bustle Digital Group
summary: A digital media company built around Bustle expanded through acquisitions (Elite Daily, Nylon, Mic's assets, Gawker's Gawker.com brand, and later Rolling Stone's parent PMC merger discussions) to become one of the larger independent digital publishers.
lesson: Acquiring distressed media brands cheaply and consolidating them onto a shared technology and sales stack can build scale that organic growth alone would not.

name: Dotdash Meredith
summary: IAC's About.com properties were rebuilt into Dotdash, a portfolio of SEO-optimized how-to and lifestyle brands, and merged with legacy publisher Meredith Corporation in 2021 to form one of the largest U.S. digital publishers by traffic.
lesson: Combining disciplined SEO-driven digital brands with a legacy publisher's print advertiser relationships and content library can create scale advantages neither had alone.

name: TechCrunch
funding: Acquired by AOL in 2010 for a reported ~$25-40M
summary: A technology-industry news blog built a dominant readership among startup founders and investors, was acquired by AOL in 2010, and continued operating profitably through subsequent ownership by Verizon and Yahoo.
lesson: Owning the trusted trade-news relationship with an entire industry's founders and investors creates a durable moat even through multiple corporate ownership changes.

name: Skift
summary: A travel-industry trade publication built a profitable B2B media business through subscriptions, research reports, and industry events, becoming a leading voice in travel-industry journalism.
lesson: A focused B2B trade-media strategy combining content, research, and events can be capital-efficient compared to consumer-scale media plays.

name: ProPublica
summary: A nonprofit investigative-journalism organization built a sustainable philanthropic funding model and won multiple Pulitzer Prizes, demonstrating a viable alternative to advertising-dependent news.
lesson: A nonprofit funding model anchored in philanthropic donors can sustain high-cost investigative journalism that ad-supported outlets increasingly cannot afford.

name: The Texas Tribune
summary: A nonprofit statehouse-news outlet built a sustainable membership and philanthropic funding model, becoming a template widely replicated by other regional nonprofit newsrooms across the U.S.
lesson: Nonprofit, membership-funded local journalism can fill the coverage gap left by shrinking newspaper newsrooms in a financially sustainable way.

name: Talking Points Memo
summary: One of the earliest independent political blogs transitioned to a reader-subscription (Prime membership) model and remained an independently profitable news operation for over two decades.
lesson: An early, loyal niche readership converted to a direct-subscription model can sustain an independent newsroom for decades without outside capital.

name: Recode/Re/code
summary: Kara Swisher and Walt Mossberg relaunched their tech-news franchise after leaving AllThingsD, and sold Re/code to Vox Media in 2015, where it continued as Recode, later folded into Vox's tech coverage.
lesson: An established journalist brand and readership relationship can be relaunched independently and successfully monetized even after losing a prior corporate home.

name: Complex Networks
funding: Acquired by BuzzFeed in 2021 for ~$300M, then sold to NTWRK/Vine Ventures/Verizon in 2023
summary: A youth culture and streetwear media brand expanded from print magazine into digital content, video, and e-commerce, and was acquired by BuzzFeed for roughly $300M in 2021 before later changing hands again.
lesson: Extending a media brand into adjacent commerce and events revenue can build enterprise value well beyond what advertising alone would support.

name: Newsela
summary: An education-focused content platform that rewrites news articles at multiple reading levels for classroom use built a large base of school-district customers and became a profitable edtech-media hybrid.
lesson: Repackaging existing news content for a well-funded institutional buyer, like school districts, can be a more durable business than competing for consumer ad dollars.

name: Scribd
summary: A document-sharing site pivoted from an ad-supported model plagued by copyright issues to a subscription e-book and audiobook reading service, becoming a profitable content-subscription business.
lesson: A troubled early business model built on legally risky user-uploaded content can be successfully rebuilt around licensed subscription content.

name: Contently
summary: A content-marketing platform connecting freelance journalists with brands built a durable enterprise SaaS and marketplace business serving corporate content teams.
lesson: Serving brands' content-marketing needs directly, rather than competing as a media publisher for ad dollars, proved a more durable niche.

name: Flipboard
summary: A visually driven content-aggregation and curation app survived multiple pivots through changing mobile and social landscapes, later embracing decentralized social protocols like ActivityPub to remain relevant.
lesson: A willingness to repeatedly reinvent the underlying distribution technology can keep a content-curation product relevant across platform-era shifts.

name: 1440 Media
summary: A daily news-summary newsletter built a very large subscriber base through an unbranded, non-partisan digest format and low-cost organic growth, reaching profitability without significant outside funding.
lesson: A stripped-down, ad-light newsletter format focused purely on utility can achieve massive organic scale with minimal capital.

name: Chartbeat
summary: A real-time web analytics company built specifically for newsrooms became a standard tool used by major publishers to track live audience engagement, sustaining a profitable B2B SaaS business.
lesson: Building analytics tooling specifically for publishers' newsroom workflows, rather than general-purpose web analytics, created a defensible enterprise niche.

name: NationSwell
summary: A media platform covering social innovation and civic solutions journalism wound down its consumer media operations around 2020, shifting fully to a private membership and consulting model.
lesson: Solutions-oriented journalism can struggle to build a large enough ad-supported audience, forcing a pivot away from public media entirely.

name: Machinima (media/blogging network)
summary: Originally a gaming-content blog and video network majority-owned by Warner Bros., it was shut down abruptly in 2019 with mass layoffs after failing to build a sustainable advertising model as YouTube's direct creator tools matured.
lesson: Intermediary content networks lose their reason to exist once the underlying platform gives creators direct monetization tools.

name: International Business Times (IBT Media)
summary: A global business-news outlet grew rapidly through an SEO-driven, high-volume publishing strategy but faced repeated fraud and traffic-manipulation allegations tied to its founders' connections to Olivet University, damaging its credibility.
lesson: An aggressive SEO-volume publishing strategy built on ties to controversial funders can generate persistent credibility and legal exposure that outlasts any traffic gains.

name: Livingly Media
summary: A women's lifestyle content network (LoveToKnow, Lonny) built through SEO-optimized articles across a portfolio of niche sites was gradually wound down and sold off in pieces as programmatic-ad rates declined.
lesson: A content-farm-style portfolio strategy dependent on programmatic display advertising is highly exposed to declining CPM rates over time.

name: TheStreet
funding: Public company (IPO 1999); acquired by TheMaven in 2019 for ~$16.5M
summary: Jim Cramer's financial-news and subscription-research site went public in 1999, weathered the dot-com crash, and remained a functioning subscription financial-media business before its 2019 acquisition by TheMaven.
lesson: A subscription financial-research product with a recognizable media personality can survive multiple market cycles that sank many dot-com-era peers.

name: AOL (content/publishing era)
funding: Public company; acquired by Verizon in 2015 for $4.4B
summary: Beyond its dial-up ISP roots, AOL built a significant digital-media publishing arm (HuffPost, TechCrunch, Engadget) that was valuable enough to be a central piece of Verizon's $4.4B acquisition in 2015.
lesson: A legacy internet company can successfully reinvent itself as a media-publishing holding company through a string of well-chosen acquisitions.

name: Newsy
summary: A video-first news outlet aimed at cord-cutters was rebranded as Scripps News and effectively wound down as a distinct national news brand in 2023 amid Scripps' broader restructuring and cost-cutting.
lesson: An innovative video-news format still needs a sustainable distribution and revenue plan independent of a parent company's shifting strategic priorities.

name: Thrillist
funding: Merged with Group Nine Media in 2016; part of Vox Media since 2024
summary: A men's lifestyle and food-and-drink newsletter and content site merged into Group Nine Media in 2016 and continued operating as a recognizable digital lifestyle brand through Vox Media's 2024 acquisition of Group Nine.
lesson: Combining several complementary digital lifestyle brands under one shared operating company can extend their commercial life well past their standalone startup phase.

name: NowThis
funding: Part of Group Nine Media, now Vox Media
summary: A mobile-first, social-video news outlet built one of the largest social video audiences of its era and became a core brand within Group Nine Media, later merged into Vox Media in 2024.
lesson: Native, platform-optimized short-form video news distribution can build reach at a scale traditional broadcast news struggled to match.

name: The Dodo
funding: Part of Group Nine Media, now Vox Media
summary: An animal-focused social video and content brand built one of the largest engaged social-media audiences in digital media and became a core Group Nine Media property, later merged into Vox Media.
lesson: A tightly focused emotional content niche, distributed natively on social platforms, can build outsized engagement relative to a small content team.

name: Mashable
funding: Sold to Ziff Davis in 2017 for ~$50M
summary: An early technology and internet-culture blog scaled into a major digital publisher, and after financial struggles was acquired by Ziff Davis in 2017 for roughly $50M, continuing to operate profitably under that ownership.
lesson: Even after a period of financial distress, an established digital media brand with a loyal audience can find a stable home under a larger publisher.

name: Engadget
funding: Part of AOL, then Verizon/Oath, then Yahoo
summary: A technology-product review and news site built a large, loyal readership and continued operating profitably across multiple corporate parents from AOL to Verizon to Yahoo.
lesson: A durable content brand focused on a stable reader need, like product reviews, can survive many changes in corporate ownership.

name: The Verge
summary: A technology, science, and culture publication launched by former Engadget staff on Vox Media's Chorus platform became one of the most-read technology news sites, sustaining Vox Media's flagship tech vertical.
lesson: A star editorial team leaving one outlet to rebuild an even stronger competing brand on better technology and backing can decisively win the audience.

name: Rooster Teeth (media/publishing arm)
summary: A pioneering internet content and podcast network with a devoted fan community was shut down in 2024 by Warner Bros. Discovery after 21 years amid broader corporate cost-cutting, despite reported profitability.
lesson: A profitable niche digital-media brand can still be cut when it doesn't fit a parent conglomerate's narrowing strategic focus.

name: Patreon (creator publishing)
summary: A membership platform letting writers, podcasters, and other creators charge subscribers directly became a foundational piece of independent media and publishing infrastructure, reaching profitability in the early 2020s.
lesson: Building payment and membership infrastructure for independent creators can become durable infrastructure even without owning any content itself.

name: Medium
summary: Evan Williams' blogging and publishing platform repeatedly pivoted its business model, from ad-supported to a metered paywall to a flat subscription, laying off staff multiple times while never matching its early venture-scale ambitions.
lesson: Repeatedly changing a publishing platform's core monetization model erodes trust with both the writers and readers it depends on.

name: WordPress.com/Automattic
summary: The commercial company behind WordPress.com and the open-source WordPress software became the dominant blogging and website-publishing platform powering a large share of the web's sites, reaching a multi-billion-dollar valuation.
lesson: Open-source foundations paired with a hosted commercial product can build a publishing-platform moat that is very difficult for closed competitors to dislodge.
